elements.sent-2411-001: Recommender systems (RS) play a critical role in delivering personalized content across various online platforms, leveraging collaborative filtering (CF) as a key technique to generate recommendations based on users' historical interaction data.
elements.sent-2411-002: Recent advancements in CF have been driven by the adoption of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), which model user-item interactions as bipartite graphs, enabling the capture of high-order collaborative signals.
elements.sent-2411-003: Despite their success, GNN-based methods face significant challenges due to the inherent popularity bias in the user-item interaction graph's topology, leading to skewed recommendations that favor popular items over less-known ones.
elements.sent-2411-004: To address this challenge, we propose a novel topology-aware popularity debiasing framework, Test-time Simplicial Propagation (TSP), which incorporates simplicial complexes (SCs) to enhance the expressiveness of GNNs.
elements.sent-2411-005: Unlike traditional methods that focus on pairwise relationships, our approach captures multi-order relationships through SCs, providing a more comprehensive representation of user-item interactions.
elements.sent-2411-006: By enriching the neighborhoods of tail items and leveraging SCs for feature smoothing, TSP enables the propagation of multi-order collaborative signals and effectively mitigates biased propagation.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our TSP module is designed as a plug-and-play solution, allowing for seamless integration into pre-trained GNN-based models without the need for fine-tuning additional parameters.
elements.sent-2411-008: Extensive experiments on five real-world datasets demonstrate the superior performance of our method, particularly in long-tail recommendation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-009: Visualization results further confirm that TSP produces more uniform distributions of item representations, leading to fairer and more accurate recommendations.
elements.sent-2411-010: Code and data will be made available.
elements.sent-2411-011: Recommender systems (RS) are essential for delivering personalized content across various online platforms.
elements.sent-2411-012: They have been widely applied in areas such as e-commerce, social media, news portals, app stores, and digital libraries~\cite{survey .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Collaborative filtering (CF) is the most crucial technique in the field of RS, employing algorithms that generate item recommendations based on the analysis of users' historical interaction data.
elements.sent-2411-014: Recent advancements of CF have been driven by the adoption of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) \cite{lightgcn, lightgcl,svdgcn .}
elements.sent-2411-015: By representing user-item interactions as a bipartite graph, GNNs are capable of capturing high-order collaborative signals between users and items through message passing and aggregation mechanisms~\cite{ngcf .}
elements.sent-2411-016: This unique advantage makes GNNs achieve state-of-the-art performance in recommendation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-017: Although GNN-based collaborative filtering has achieved significant success, a critical issue has emerged concerning the popularity bias inherent in the user-item interaction graph's topology and the approaches used to mitigate this bias.
elements.sent-2411-018: The bias issue poses a significant challenge in RS, arising from various factors such as user behavior, item popularity, and system design.
elements.sent-2411-019: Biases are categorized based on their underlying causes, including exposure bias, selection bias, popularity bias, and others~\cite{debias .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Among these, popularity bias is one of the most prevalent and difficult to address in RS.
elements.sent-2411-021: It refers to the tendency of RS to recommend popular items over more relevant ones.
elements.sent-2411-022: Popularity bias occurs because, in real-world datasets, a small number of items are typically highly popular, while the majority are less well-known or niche~\cite{pareto .}
elements.sent-2411-023: As a result, the model's learned representations will exhibit a bias towards popular items.
elements.sent-2411-024: There is a growing body of research focused on developing techniques to alleviate popularity bias, including regularization~\cite{reg1 , adversarial learning~\cite{adv1 , and causal learning~\cite{casual1 methods.}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: While these approaches have shown promise in alleviating popularity bias, they often fail to explicitly address the inherent biases embedded in the graph topology itself.
elements.sent-2411-026: GNN-based recommendation methods rely on message passing and aggregation mechanisms to learn representations of users and item.
elements.sent-2411-027: A recent study~\cite{amplify notes that popular items exert a dominant influence on the information propagation among neighboring users in GNNs.}
elements.sent-2411-028: This dominance results in a representation space where users are drawn closer to popular items, regardless of their actual preferences.
elements.sent-2411-030: We select two typical GNN-based recommendation methods, LightGCN~\cite{lightgcn and SimGCL~\cite{simgcl trained on the Gowalla dataset, project the representations of the top 500 popular items, as well as the top 500 long-tail items, into a two-dimensional space.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: It shows that user representations tend to cluster closer to popular items while remaining more distant from long-tail items, resulting in a lower recommendation probability for long-tail items in GNN-based models.
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{minipage}{.23\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figs/lgn.pdf} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{.23\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figs/simgcl.pdf} \end{minipage} \caption{Representation distribution of users, popular items and tail items on Gowalla dataset.} \label{fig:case_emb}
elements.sent-2411-032: A line of research \cite{topo1, topo2, topo3 attempts to bridge the gap between popular and tail items by applying topological graph augmentations, directly addressing the root cause of popularity bias from the perspective of graph topology.}
elements.sent-2411-033: However, augmentation-based methods still rely on pairwise relationships, and predominantly focus on message passing over the augmented graphs, overlooking the rich higher-order relationships embedded within them.
elements.sent-2411-034: As a result, the significant disparity between the degrees of popular and tail items persists during the message passing process.
elements.sent-2411-035: Recognizing these shortcomings, researchers have started exploring methods that explicitly model higher-order relationships in RS.
elements.sent-2411-036: Hypergraph-based approaches, such as HCCF \cite{hccf , represent a significant advancement in this direction.}
elements.sent-2411-037: By employing hyperedges, these methods can capture complex interactions involving multiple users and items simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-038: This enhanced expressiveness enables a more nuanced representation of user-item relationships, potentially leading to more accurate and less biased recommendations.
elements.sent-2411-039: However, while hypergraphs offer greater expressiveness, they also introduce scalability challenges due to their ability to connect an arbitrary number of nodes, resulting in significant computational overhead, particularly in large-scale RS \cite{hypergraph .}
elements.sent-2411-040: To address the challenging issue of popularity bias in GNN-based methods and overcome the shortcomings of existing mainstream approaches, we propose a novel topology-aware popularity debiasing framework called Test-time Simplicial Propagation (TSP).
elements.sent-2411-041: Our core idea is to construct simplicial complexes (SCs) for nodes, enhancing their representations through hierarchical multi-order connections.
elements.sent-2411-042: Figure \ref{fig:topo illustrates the difference in message passing between augmented graph, hypergraph, and simplicial complexes.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Simplicial complexes extend beyond pairwise connections by capturing multi-order relationships, offering a more nuanced and comprehensive representation of user-item interactions.
elements.sent-2411-044: Specifically, we begin by enriching the neighborhoods of tail items using graph augmentation to achieve a more balanced topological structure.
elements.sent-2411-045: We then introduce SCs to model complex and multi-dimensional relationships between nodes, capturing interactions such as shared user preferences or co-consumption patterns.
elements.sent-2411-046: Subsequently, we employ message passing on these SCs to generate embeddings that integrate information from various levels of relationships, further mitigating biased propagation.
elements.sent-2411-047: Finally, these representations, functioning as a plug-and-play module, are integrated with the original learned representations to serve as fair representations of users and items for recommendation.

elements.sent-2411-048: We summarize our contributions as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-049: We propose a novel topology-aware debiasing method by incorporating simplicial complexes.
elements.sent-2411-050: Our method addresses the bias propagation issue in existing GNN-based recommendation methods, which arises from popularity-induced biases in the user-item network topology during message passing and aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-051: By leveraging simplicial complexes for feature smoothing, our method enables the propagation of multi-order collaborative signals and effectively achieves feature debiasing.
elements.sent-2411-052: Our proposed Test-time Simplicial Propagation (TSP) module is designed as a plug-and-play solution, enabling seamless integration into pre-trained GNN-based models for inference without the need for fine-tuning additional parameters, thereby demonstrating its superior extensibility.
elements.sent-2411-053: We conduct extensive experiments on five real-world datasets, varying in size, domain, and sparsity.
elements.sent-2411-054: The proposed method exhibits remarkable performance, particularly in long-tail recommendation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-055: Visualization results indicate that our approach produces more uniform distributions of user and item representations, confirming its ability to produce fairer representations.
elements.sent-2411-056: GNNs \cite{chebynet,gcn,gat have achieved significant success in various downstream tasks involving graph data.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Many works \cite{gcmc, stargcn, gnn1, gnn2 have applied GNNs to recommender systems.}
elements.sent-2411-058: For example, NGCF \cite{ngcf models user-item interactions as a bipartite graph and utilizes graph convolutional layers to learn embeddings for users and items.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Although GNNs are effective in capturing collaborative filtering signals, their core message passing and aggregation mechanisms may introduce unnecessary redundancy.

elements.sent-2411-068: Chen et al.~ \cite{substruct further show that GNNs have difficulty in detecting substructures like triangles and cliques.}
elements.sent-2411-069: To address these limitations, some research works begin to explore the use of simplicial complexes to enhance GNNs.
elements.sent-2411-070: Simplicial complexes are first explored from a signal processing perspective \cite{simplicialsignal .}
elements.sent-2411-071: Recent works \cite{sann,mpsn, hsn propose to conduct message passing on simplicial complexes.}
elements.sent-2411-072: Simplicial Neural Network (SNN) \cite{snn extends graph convolution to higher orders by utilizing Hodge Laplacians.}
elements.sent-2411-073: BSCNet \cite{bscnets , a representative model that enables message passing across arbitrary rank, integrates node- and edge-level shifting mechanisms to predict linkages among nodes using a pseudo Hodge Laplacian.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Subsequent works \cite{san1, san2, san3 draw inspiration from the graph attention mechanism introduced in GAT \cite{gat and incorporate a simplicial attention mechanism to enhance message passing between simplices.}}

elements.sent-2411-076: One straightforward approach is to apply regularizations \cite{reg1, reg2, reg3, debias1 to balance the influence of popular items.}

elements.sent-2411-078: Adversarial learning is also a widely adopted method in popularity debiasing \cite{debias,adv1, adv2, adv3 .}
elements.sent-2411-079: The main idea is to co-train another adversarial network to improve the fairness of the recommendation.
elements.sent-2411-080: Another perspective is to model the popularity bias using casual graph \cite{macr,casual1,casual2,casual3 .}
elements.sent-2411-081: Counterfactual reasoning is then applied to causal graphs to mitigate the bias.
elements.sent-2411-082: MACR \cite{macr , for example, models the effect of popular items on the recommendation process and removes their influence in the final inference.}

elements.sent-2411-097: Given the set of vertices $\mathcal{V}$, a $k$-simplex $\sigma \subseteq \mathcal{V}$ is a subset containing $k+1$ elements, where any two distinct elements in the subset are connected by an edge.
elements.sent-2411-098: For example, a 0-simplex is a vertex, a 1-simplex is an edge, and a 2-simplex is a triangle.

elements.sent-2411-102: In the following sections, we provide a detailed description of each part.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{figs/tsp_new.pdf} \caption{The proposed Test-time Simplicial Propagation (TSP) framework compromises three components: (a) Tail Enhancement that augments the original graph based on node similarity. (b) Embedding Converter that calculates embeddings for all simplices in augmented graph $G_{aug}$ based on pre-trained node embedding. (c) Simplicial Propagation that performs parallel message passing for each order of simplicial complexes and concatenates multi-order embeddings for recommendation.} \label{fig:framework}
elements.sent-2411-103: To promote a balanced graph topology and improve the adjacency of tail items, we begin by expanding the original graph $G$ to enhanced graph $G_{aug}$ through the incorporation of latent edges between nodes, determined by similarity metrics: \begin{equation}\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X},\end{equation} where $\mathbf{S}$ represents the similarity matrix and $\mathbf{X}$ is the node embedding matrix.

elements.sent-2411-105: Here, $\mathcal{E _{add}$ and $\mathcal{E _{ori}$ denote the sets of added latent edges and the original graph's edges, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-106: The relationships between neighboring simplices are described using the boundary operator $\partial_k: \mathcal{X ^k \rightarrow \mathcal{X ^{k-1}$, which maps a $k$-dimensional simplex to its $(k-1)$-dimensional boundary simplex.}}
elements.sent-2411-107: This operator is mathematically represented by a boundary matrix $\mathbf{B _k \in \{0,1\}^{|\mathcal{X ^{k-1 | \times |\mathcal{X ^k|}$, defined as follows: \begin{equation}\mathbf{B}_k(i,j) = \begin{cases} 1, \text{if } \sigma_i\subset \sigma_j, \text{ for } (k-1)\text{-simplex } \sigma_i \text{ and } k\text{-simplex } \sigma_j 0, \text{otherwise} \end{cases}\end{equation} This schema is extended to capture the relationships between simplices of different orders: \begin{equation}\mathbf{B}_{m, n} = \prod_{i=m+1}^n \mathbf{B}_i,\end{equation} where $\mathbf{B _{m, n}$ describes the adjacency between simplices of order $m$ and $n$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-108: Adjacency relations form the basis for embedding conversion in TSP.
elements.sent-2411-109: Initial embeddings for various orders of simplices are derived from node embeddings obtained using pretrained graph neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-110: For example, each $k$-dimensional simplex $\sigma_k$ is represented as the aggregation of the embeddings of its constituent elements: \begin{equation}\mathbf{x}_{\sigma_k} = \sum_{i\in \sigma_k} \mathbf{x}_i.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-111: The equivalent matrix form is: \begin{equation}\mathbf{X}_k = (\prod_{i=1}^k \mathbf{B}_i)^T \mathbf{X},\end{equation} where $\mathbf{X _k$ and $\mathbf{X}$ are the embedding matrices for $k$-simplices and nodes, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-113: Accordingly, the embedding for each node contained within $k$-order simplicial complexes (SCs) is: \begin{equation}\mathbf{X}_{0\leftarrow k} = (\prod_{i=1}^k \mathbf{B}_i) \mathbf{X}_k \label{eq:convert}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-114: Boundary matrices form the foundation for defining Hodge Laplacians, which extend graph Laplacians to accommodate simplicial complexes: \begin{equation}L_0 = B_1B_1^T, L_k = B_{k+1}B_{k+1}^T + B_k^TB_k, \quad where \ k=1,2,...,K-1. L_K = B_K^TB_K.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation}L_0 = B_1B_1^T, L_k = B_{k+1}B_{k+1}^T + B_k^TB_k, \quad where \ k=1,2,...,K-1. L_K = B_K^TB_K.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-115: Each Hodge Laplacian $\mathbf{L _k$ comprises an upper Laplacian $\mathbf{L _{k, u = \mathbf{B _{k+1}\mathbf{B _{k+1 ^T$ and a lower Laplacian $\mathbf{L _{k, l = \mathbf{B _k^T\mathbf{B _k$, aside from $\mathbf{L _0$ and $\mathbf{L _K$, which is specifically the case for $\mathbf{L _0$ aligned with the graph Laplacian \cite{laplacian .}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-116: Figure ~\ref{fig:sc exhibits an example of boundary matrices and Hodge Laplacians for simplicial complexes.}
elements.sent-2411-117: The diagonal degree matrix for Hodge Laplacian is defined as follows: \begin{equation}\mathbf{D}_k(i, i) = \sum_j \mathbf{L}_k(i, j).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-118: Similar to normalized graph Laplacian, the normalized Hodge Laplacian is expressed by: \begin{equation}\tilde{\mathbf{L}_k} = \mathbf{D}_k^{-\frac{1}{2}} \mathbf{L}_k \mathbf{D}_k^{-\frac{1}{2}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-119: Our approach integrates both node and multi-order simplicial complex propagation.
elements.sent-2411-120: We formulate an efficient propagation scheme using Hodge Laplacians, drawing inspiration from graph propagation techniques.
elements.sent-2411-121: The message-passing rule for $k$-order simplicial complexes (SCs) is established as follows: \begin{equation}\mathbf{X}^{(l+1)}_k = (\mathbf{I}-\tilde{\mathbf{L}_k})\mathbf{X}^{(l)}_k, \label{eq:lap}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{I}$ represents the identity matrix, and $\mathbf{X ^{(l) _k$ denotes the embeddings of $k$-simplices at layer $l$.}}
elements.sent-2411-122: Equation \ref{eq:lap illustrates a generalized form of Laplacian smoothing, similar to graph convolution~\cite{lapconv .}}
elements.sent-2411-123: When $k=0$, this process corresponds to conventional node message passing on graphs.
elements.sent-2411-124: The embedding for each simplicial order is computed as the mean of embeddings across each message passing layer: \begin{equation}\mathbf{X}_k = \text{MEAN}(\mathbf{X}_k^{(0)}, \mathbf{X}_k^{(1)}, ..., \mathbf{X}_k^{(L)}),\end{equation} where $L$ represents the maximum message-passing layer.
elements.sent-2411-125: Subsequently, embeddings from all orders are concatenated for recommendation tasks: \begin{equation}\mathbf{X}_{rec} = \text{CONCAT}(\mathbf{X}_0, \mathbf{X}_{0\leftarrow 2}, \mathbf{X}_{0\leftarrow 3}, ..., \mathbf{X}_{0\leftarrow K}),\end{equation} where $\mathbf{X _{rec}$ denotes the output embedding for recommendation, $K$ is the highest order of simplices, and $\mathbf{X _{0\leftarrow k}$ is derived using Equation \ref{eq:convert .}}}

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Test-time Simplicial Propagation} \label{alg:sp} \KwIn{Pretrained node embedding $\mathbf{X}_0^{(0)}$ Boundary matrices $\mathbf{B}_k$ Hodge Laplacians $\tilde{\mathbf{L}_k}$ Number of propagation layers $L$ Maxium SC order $K$ } \KwOut{final node embeddings $\mathbf{X}$} \For{$k=1$ to $K$}{ \tcc*[f]{Initialize multi-order simplex embeddings} $\mathbf{X}_k^{(0)} \gets (\prod_{i=1}^k \mathbf{B}_i)^T \mathbf{X}_0^{(0)}$ } \For{$k=0$ to $K$}{ \tcc*[f]{Propagate embeddings for each simplex order} \For{$l=1$ to $L$}{ $\mathbf{X}_k^{(l+1)} \gets (I-\tilde{\mathbf{L}_k})\mathbf{X}_k^{(l)}$ } } \For{$k=0$ to $K$}{ \tcc*[f]{Aggregate embeddings across layers} $\mathbf{X}_k \gets \text{MEAN}(\mathbf{X}_k^{(1)}, \mathbf{X}_k^{(2)}, \ldots, \mathbf{X}_k^{(L)})$ } \For{$k=1$ to $K$}{\tcc*[f]{Convert higher-order simplex embeddings to node embeddings} $\mathbf{X}_{0\leftarrow k} \gets (\prod_{i=1}^k \mathbf{B}_i) \mathbf{X}_{k}$ } $\mathbf{X} \gets \text{CONCAT}(\mathbf{X}_0, \mathbf{X}_{0\leftarrow 1}, \ldots, \mathbf{X}_{0\leftarrow K})$ \Return $\mathbf{X}$

elements.sent-2411-134: We use five real-world benchmark datasets in our experiments: Yelp2018\footnote{\url{https://www.yelp.com/dataset and Gowalla\footnote{\url{https://snap.stanford.edu/data/loc-gowalla.html are widely used datasets in the RS research, Adressa\footnote{\url{https://reclab.idi.ntnu.no/dataset and Globo\footnote{\url{https://www.kaggle.com/gspmoreira/news-portal-user-interactions-by-globocom are two popular news recommendation datasets, ML10M\footnote{\url{https://grouplens.org/datasets/movielens/10m contains movie ratings from MovieLens.}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-138: Conventional train-test split strategies are not suitable for evaluating the debiasing performance of recommender systems, as test sets created through random partitioning inevitably retain the same popularity biases present in the original data.
elements.sent-2411-139: To accurately assess a model's ability to mitigate popularity bias and highlight relevant long-tail items, we need unbiased test sets with a balanced distribution across the popularity spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-140: To this end, we adopt the unbiased data split method proposed by He et al. \cite{macr .}
elements.sent-2411-141: Specifically, we uniformly sample 10\% of the user-item interactions for the test set, another 10\% for the validation set, and the remaining 80\% for the training set.
elements.sent-2411-142: Importantly, all items are sampled with equal probability during this process, regardless of their overall popularity in the original data.
elements.sent-2411-143: This stratified sampling scheme ensures that the test and validation sets are well-balanced, covering the entire spectrum of item popularity.
elements.sent-2411-144: We evaluate the performance of our method on these unbiased test sets.
elements.sent-2411-145: The results are reported as the average across all users.
elements.sent-2411-146: We choose three recent advanced GNN-based methods as backbone models.
elements.sent-2411-147: LightGCN~\cite{lightgcn employs lightweight graph convolutions for recommendation.}

elements.sent-2411-191: In this work, we propose a novel Test-time Simplicial Propagation (TSP) method to address topology bias caused by item popularity in GNN-based recommendation.
elements.sent-2411-192: Our key contribution is the development of a plug-and-play module that employs a message-passing scheme over simplicial complexes for debiasing.
elements.sent-2411-193: Designed for test-time inference, our method is both efficient and highly scalable.
elements.sent-2411-194: Our promising results highlight the significant potential of simplicial complexes as a valuable inductive bias for debiasing and enhancing collaborative filtering with graph neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-195: We believe that our work opens up exciting avenues for future research in leveraging simplicial complexes for recommender systems.. { \bibliographystyle{ACM-Reference-Format.}}
elements.sent-2411-196: Graph-based recommender systems have achieved remarkable effectiveness by modeling high-order interactions between users and items.
elements.sent-2411-197: However, such approaches are significantly undermined by popularity bias, which distorts the interaction graph's structure—referred to as topology bias.
elements.sent-2411-198: This leads to overrepresentation of popular items, thereby reinforcing biases and fairness issues through the user-system feedback loop.
elements.sent-2411-199: Despite attempts to study this effect, most prior work focuses on the embedding or gradient level bias, overlooking how topology bias fundamentally distorts the message passing process itself.
elements.sent-2411-200: We bridge this gap by providing an empirical and theoretical analysis from a Dirichlet energy perspective, revealing that graph message passing inherently amplifies topology bias and consistently benefits highly connected nodes.
elements.sent-2411-201: To address these limitations, we propose Test-time Simplicial Propagation~(\model), which extends message passing to higher-order simplicial complexes.
elements.sent-2411-202: By incorporating richer structures beyond pairwise connections, \model mitigates harmful topology bias and substantially improves the representation and recommendation of long-tail items during inference.
elements.sent-2411-203: Extensive experiments across five real-world datasets demonstrate the superiority of our approach in mitigating topology bias and enhancing recommendation quality.
elements.sent-2411-204: Recommender systems~(RS) are fundamental components of modern online platforms, playing a crucial role in connecting users with relevant items~\cite{intro1, intro2, intro3 .}
elements.sent-2411-205: Recently, graph-based methods, such as LightGCN~\cite{lgn , have gained prominence in RS, as they effectively capture collaborative filtering signals by modeling high-order connectivity between users and items~\cite{gr1, gr2, gr3, gr4 .}}
elements.sent-2411-206: Despite their effectiveness, graph-based methods suffer from popularity bias, where popular items are recommended disproportionately more often than less popular items~\cite{pop1, pop2 .}
elements.sent-2411-207: This notorious effect not only undermines the accuracy and fairness of recommendation~\cite{div, tail1, tail2 , but also exacerbates the Matthew Effect and the filter bubble~\cite{bubble1, bubble2 through the user-system feedback loop.}}
elements.sent-2411-208: In graph-based recommender systems, popularity bias mainly distorts the topology of the interaction graph, producing a highly biased degree distribution as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:bias (a), which we refer to as topology bias.}
elements.sent-2411-209: While research has shown that the topology bias gets further amplified~\cite{macr, amp in graph-based methods, the root cause of this effect remains rather unexplored.}
elements.sent-2411-210: Although some recent studies have attempted to understand this issue, they exhibit significant limitations.
elements.sent-2411-211: Their analyses focus mainly on the embedding landscape~\cite{emb1, emb2 or gradient bias~\cite{amplify, grad2, grad3 after message passing.}}
elements.sent-2411-212: The question of how topology bias distorts the message passing process remains unsolved.
elements.sent-2411-213: Our empirical and theoretical analysis reveals the following results: (1) The graph message passing mechanism is equivalent to minimizing the global graph Dirichlet energy.
elements.sent-2411-214: Based on the above studies, we propose Test-time Simplicial Propagation~(\model), which performs message passing over simplicial complexes with intra- and inter-simplex smoothing to avoid the dominance of certain nodes or substructures.
elements.sent-2411-215: By incorporating simplices instead of mere edges, our proposed \model mitigates harmful topology bias during inference, improving the performance of tail nodes without requiring RS retraining.
elements.sent-2411-217: We provide comprehensive theoretical analysis of how topology bias distorts message passing in graph-based recommender systems.
elements.sent-2411-218: We establish formal connections between graph message passing, Dirichlet energy optimization, and node embeddings, offering a mathematical framework to understand why popular nodes are systematically favored.
elements.sent-2411-219: We propose a principled approach to overcome the limitations of pairwise edge message passing by introducing \model, which leverages higher-order simplicial complexes for information propagation, enabling more balanced representation learning across nodes of varying popularity.
elements.sent-2411-220: We develop \model as a plug-and-play solution that operates solely during inference, making it compatible with existing recommender systems without requiring costly retraining.
elements.sent-2411-221: Through extensive experiments across five diverse real-world datasets, we demonstrate that \model can effectively mitigate topology bias without impairing the overall recommendation quality.
elements.sent-2411-222: Our results show consistent performance gains in tail nodes as well as competitive or better results in overall recommendation compared to state-of-the-art baselines.

elements.section-2411-007: Topology Bias from Dirichlet Energy Perspective
elements.sent-2411-244: In this section, we provide a theoretical analysis of how topology bias shapes the message passing process in graph-based recommender systems.
elements.sent-2411-245: Our analysis uncovers the mechanism by which popular nodes are systematically favored, a phenomenon rooted in an energy minimization dynamic.
elements.subsection-2411-016: Message Passing as Dirichlet Energy Optimization
elements.sent-2411-246: We begin by establishing a fundamental equivalence between graph message passing and a Dirichlet energy minimization problem.
elements.sent-2411-247: This equivalence allows us to understand how the underlying graph topology directly governs information propagation.
elements.sent-2411-248: Lemma~\ref{lemma:equiv reveals that the message passing operation implicitly solves a global smoothness objective.}
elements.sent-2411-249: Topology bias directly alters this energy landscape, thus shaping the resulting embeddings in a degree-dependent manner, as shown by the positive correlation in Figure~\ref{fig:bias (b).}
elements.subsection-2411-017: Effect of Topology Bias on Embedding Norms
elements.sent-2411-250: Next, we characterize how embeddings after message passing reflect the topology bias.
elements.sent-2411-251: Specifically, we show that nodes with higher degrees unavoidably accumulate larger embedding norms, a dynamic we refer to as norm concentration.
elements.sent-2411-252: Its squared norm is lower bounded by its degree $d_v$: \begin{equation}\|\mathbf{x}^*_v\|_2^2 \geq d_v \cdot C(\tilde{\mathbf{L}}, \mathbf{X}^{(0)}),\end{equation} where $C$ depends on the graph Laplacian $\tilde{\mathbf{L }$ and initial embedding $\mathbf{X ^{(0)}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-253: Corollary~\ref{col:norm formalizes that popular nodes (with larger $d_v$) necessarily acquire larger norms during message passing, often resulting in higher recommendation scores.}
elements.sent-2411-254: This effect is also noted by~\citet{emb2 in their empirical observations.}
elements.subsection-2411-018: Topology Bias in Embedding Update Dynamics
elements.sent-2411-255: We further show how topology bias persists and is amplified at every message passing step by bounding each node's embedding update in terms of its local graph energy.
elements.sent-2411-256: Lemma~\ref{lemma:update exposes a self-reinforcing cycle: since the Dirichlet energy $E(v)$ itself is greater for higher-degree (popular) nodes as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:bias (b), these nodes receive larger embedding updates at each propagation step and potentially aggregate more information.}}
elements.subsection-2411-019: Hodge Decomposition and Simplicial Dirichlet Energy
elements.sent-2411-257: Theorem~\ref{theorm:hodge_decomposition shows that any $k$-order simplicial signal $\mathbf{x _{\sigma^k}$ can be decomposed~\cite{signal_decompose : \begin{equation}\mathbf{x}_{\sigma^k} = \mathbf{x}_{\sigma^k, G}+\mathbf{x}_{\sigma^k, H}+\mathbf{x}_{\sigma^k, C}, \label{eq:decompose}\end{equation} where the gradient component $\mathbf{x _{\sigma^k, G =\mathbf{B _k^T\mathbf{x _{\sigma^{k-1 }$~(corresponding to Equation~\ref{eq:low2high ), curl component $\mathbf{x _{\sigma^k, C =\mathbf{B _{k+1}\mathbf{x _{\sigma^{k+1 }$~(corresponding to Equation~\ref{eq:high2low ), and the harmonic component $\mathbf{x _{\sigma^k, H}$ satisfies $\mathbf{L _k\mathbf{x _{\sigma^k, H =\mathbf{0}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-258: This decomposition provides theoretical guidance for the interaction and conversion between simplicial signals of different orders.
elements.sent-2411-259: Different from the Dirichlet energy defined on a graph, the simplicial Dirichlet energy consists of two terms: energy between simplices sharing the same lower order component simplices~(lower simplices) and energy between simplices consisting of the same higher order simplices~(higher simplices).
elements.sent-2411-260: Consider optimizing the simplicial Dirichlet energy similar to Equation~\ref{eq:energy_op : \begin{equation}\min_{\mathbf{X}_{\sigma^k}}\quad \frac{1}{2}E_{\sigma^k}(\mathbf{X}_{\sigma_i^k})+\frac{\mu}{2}\norm{\mathbf{X}_{\sigma^k}-\mathbf{X}_{\sigma^k}^{(0)}}_2^2.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-261: The gradient with respect to $\mathbf{X _{\sigma^k}$ is: \begin{equation}\nabla_{\mathbf{X}_{\sigma^k}}=\mathbf{B}_k^T\mathbf{B}_k \mathbf{X}_{\sigma^k}+\mathbf{B}_{k+1}\mathbf{B}_{k+1}^T \mathbf{X}_{\sigma^k}+\mu(\mathbf{X}_{\sigma^k}-\mathbf{X}_{\sigma^k}^{(0)})=\mathbf{L}_k\mathbf{X}_{\sigma^k}+\mu(\mathbf{X}_{\sigma^k}-\mathbf{X}_{\sigma^k}^{(0)}).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-262: One gradient descent step with learning rate $\eta$ can be written as: \begin{equation}\mathbf{X}_{\sigma^k}^{(1)}=\mathbf{X}_{\sigma^k}^{(0)}-\eta\nabla_{\mathbf{X}_{\sigma^k}}=\mathbf{X}_{\sigma^k}^{(0)}-\eta\mathbf{L}_k\mathbf{X}_{\sigma^k}^{(0)}=(\mathbf{I}-\eta\mathbf{L}_k)\mathbf{X}_{\sigma^k}^{(0)}. \label{eq:simplicial_energy_opt}\end{equation} Notably, Equation~\ref{eq:simplicial_energy_opt is the simplicial version of graph message passing.}
elements.sent-2411-263: The orthonormal eigen-decomposition of $\mathbf{L _k$ can be written as: \begin{equation}\mathbf{L}_k=\sum_{i=1}^N\lambda_i\mathbf{u}_i\mathbf{u}_i^T,\end{equation} where $0=\lambda_1=\ldots=\lambda_r<\lambda_{r+1 <\ldots\leq\lambda_N$ are the eigenvalues and $\mathbf{u _i$ are the corresponding eigenvectors.}}}
elements.sent-2411-264: Similarly, we can define a resolvent to this optimization problem as in Equation~\ref{eq:resolve : \begin{equation}\mathbf{R}_k=\mu(\mathbf{L}_k+\mu\mathbf{I})^{-1}.\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-266: Now we show how simplicial message passing can alleviate the topology bias.
elements.sent-2411-267: Lemma~\ref{lemma:simp_bias reveals that in simplicial message passing, for any embedding in the kernel of the upper adjacency operator ($\mathrm{ker (\mathbf{B _{k+1 )$), the norm is strictly reduced by the resolvent operator $\mathbf{R _k$.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-309: MACR~\citep{macr employs counterfactual reasoning based on a causal graph to mitigate popularity bias.}

elements.sent-2411-346: This paper addresses topology bias in graph-based recommender systems by analyzing message passing through a Dirichlet energy perspective.
elements.sent-2411-347: Based on our theoretical analysis, we propose Test-time Simplicial Propagation (\model) to address this bias by using higher-order simplicial complex structures for more balanced message passing.
elements.sent-2411-348: Extensive experiments demonstrate that \model effectively reduces the topology bias and improves recommendation quality.. \bibliographystyle{plainnat .}

elements.sent-2411-359: We adopt the unbiased evaluation protocol from~\citet{macr .}
elements.sent-2411-360: Specifically, we uniformly sample 10\% of interactions for the test set and 10\% for validation, ensuring all items have an equal chance of being sampled, regardless of their popularity.

elements.sent-2411-363: Recall@k is the proportion of relevant items found in the top-k recommendations.
elements.sent-2411-364: A higher Recall@k value indicates that the system is better at identifying and ranking relevant items for users.
elements.sent-2411-365: Formally, Recall@k is defined as: \begin{equation}\text{Recall@k}=\frac{\text{Number of Relevant Items in Top-k}}{\text{Total Number of Relevant Items}}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-043: \begin{equation}Recall@k=\frac{Number of Relevant Items in Top-k}{Total Number of Relevant Items}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-366: NDCG@k is a widely used evaluation metric in recommender systems to measure the ranking quality of suggested items.
elements.sent-2411-367: NDCG@k takes into account both the relevance of recommendations and their positions in the ranked list, assigning higher importance to relevant items appearing earlier.
elements.sent-2411-368: To compute NDCG@k, first calculate the Discounted Cumulative Gain (DCG) up to position k, then normalize it by the ideal DCG (IDCG) obtained from the perfect ranking.
elements.sent-2411-369: Formally, for a list of recommendations $r_1,r_2,\ldots,r_k$, the DCG@k is defined as: \begin{equation}\text{DCG@k}=\sum_{i=1}^k\frac{2^{rel_i}-1}{log_2(i+1)},\end{equation} where $rel_i$ is the relevance score of each item at position $i$.
elements.sent-2411-370: Then NDCG@k is the computed as: \begin{equation}\text{NDCG@k}=\frac{\text{DCG@k}}{\text{IDCG@k}},\end{equation} where IDCG@k is the maximum possible DCG@k for the ideal ordering of items.

elements.sent-2411-372: In a simplicial complex, the boundary of a $k$-simplex maps to its $(k-1)$-simplices (\ie the faces of the simplex).
elements.sent-2411-373: For a $k$-simplex $\sigma = \{v_0, v_1, \ldots, v_k\}$, the boundary $\partial \sigma$ is given by: \begin{equation}\label{rule} \partial \sigma = \sum_{i=0}^{k} (-1)^i \{v_0, v_1, \ldots, \hat{v_i}, \ldots, v_k\},\end{equation} where $\hat{v_i}$ indicates that vertex $v_i$ is omitted.
elements.sent-2411-374: This rule also determines the orientation of simplices in a SC boundary matrix as $-1$ or $1$, depending on the omitted $i$ of lower simplices.

elements.algorithm-2411-002: [H] \caption{Test-time Simplicial Propagation} \label{alg:sp} \KwIn{Pretrained node embedding $\mathbf{X}_0$ Boundary matrices $\mathbf{B}_k$ Hodge Laplacians ${\mathbf{L}_k}$ Number of propagation layers $L$ Simplicial filter coefficient $\beta$ Maximum SC order $K$ } \KwOut{final node embeddings $\mathbf{X}_{rec}$} \For{$k=1$ to $K$}{ \tcc*[f]{Intra-Simplex Smoothing} $\mathbf{X}_{\sigma^k}^{(0)} \gets (\prod_{i=1}^k \mathbf{B}_i^T)\,\mathbf{X}_0$ } \For{$k=0$ to $K$}{ \tcc*[f]{Inter-Simplex Propagation} \For{$l=1$ to $L$}{ $\mathbf{X}_{\sigma^k}^{(l+1)} \gets (\mathbf{I}-\beta{\mathbf{L}_k})\,\mathbf{X}_{\sigma^k}^{(l)}$ } } \For{$k=1$ to $K$}{\tcc*[f]{Multi-order Fusion} $\mathbf{X}_{0\leftarrow k} \gets (\prod_{i=1}^k \mathbf{B}_i) \,\mathbf{X}_{\sigma^k}^{(L)}$ } $\mathbf{X}_{rec} \gets \mathbf{X}_0+\text{Mean}(\mathbf{X}_{0\leftarrow 1}, \ldots, \mathbf{X}_{0\leftarrow K})$ \Return $\mathbf{X}_{rec}$

elements.sent-2411-392: The implementation of lifting graphs into (clique) simplicial complexes centers on finding every clique in the graph, where a clique is a subset of vertices with every pair connected by an edge.
elements.sent-2411-393: Efficient algorithms such as Bron–Kerbosch are often employed for this task.
elements.sent-2411-394: Each detected clique of $k$ vertices is then interpreted as a simplex of dimension $k-1$, so, for example, a triangle in the graph (a 3-clique) becomes a 2-dimensional simplex in the complex.
elements.sent-2411-395: To ensure the resulting structure is a simplicial complex, not only the maximal cliques but also all their subcliques should be included, since every subset of a clique is itself a clique and thus forms a lower-dimensional simplex.
elements.sent-2411-396: We simplify this complicated process with TopoNetX~\cite{toponetx , a suite specially designed to deal with topological data.}
elements.sent-2411-397: In this paper we focus on addressing the topology bias in recommender systems, ensuring fairer recommendation results.
elements.sent-2411-398: This can directly contribute to the development of more equitable information access and distribution, promoting diversity and inclusivity in user experiences across various platforms.
elements.sent-2411-399: However, our approach is not without its limitations.
elements.sent-2411-400: Our current model solely concentrates on analyzing simplified graph message passing without incorporating weights or non-linear activations.
elements.sent-2411-401: While this simplification is often adopted to simplify the analysis on graph message passing, this may lead to incomplete understanding of how topology bias influences this process.
elements.sent-2411-402: We leave this for future work.
